figures.caption: Figure 1: TCER computes rewards using both base and actor models, emphasizing high-entropy meaningful content over EndoR. After RL Training, TCER shows gentler entropy decay with enhanced exploration.
Table 2: Effectiveness of TCER for existing finetuned models. Applying our RL methods to finetuned models DeepWriter-8B and LongWriter-8B.
Figure 5: RL training dynamics on writing tasks. For each dataset, we report the EndoR reward trajectory, the TCER reward trajectory , and a direct entropy comparison between EndoR and TCER .
Figure 6: RL training dynamics on mathematical reasoning tasks. We report EndoR and TCER reward trajectories together with entropy comparisons for each model configuration.
Figure 16: English writing case (EndoR). Response generated by the EndoR-trained model under the same prompt.
Figure 17: English writing case (TCER). Response generated by the TCER-trained model under the same prompt.
Table 3: Performance comparison on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. TCER outperforms both the SFT baseline and EndoR across different base models.
Figure 19: Aggregation prompt used by Gemini 2.5 Pro to consolidate highlighted sentences across judges via agreement-based selection.

Figure 18: Sentence-selection prompt used by individual judge models (GPT-4o, Claude Opus 4, and Gemini 2.5 Pro). Each judge independently selects at least five highlighted sentences from the same output.

Figure 2: Overview of TCER training pipeline and reward comparison. (a) Training workflow: SFT on high-quality data to obtain πs, followed by Reference Augmented GRPO with TCER that incorporate information gain ϕ and gating weight w. (b) Sentence-level reward visualization on a rollout: token-level rewards are averaged within each sentence comparing TCER and EndoR. (Rewards are average log-probs; higher is closer to 0.)
Figure 11: English reward case study: sentence-level EndoR vs. TCER rewards. The table reports ∆ = TCER− EndoR and whether each line is selected by the judge panel.
Figure 10: English reward case study: prompt and model output.
Figure 4: Displays these trajectories as line plots, with EndoR scores shown in blue and TCER scores in orange. High-quality sentences identified by our judge panel are marked with bold red points.
Table 5: Ablation studies on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with the DeepWriting dataset. Each component contributes to the overall performance.
Figure 12: Writing case (SFT). Response generated by the SFT-only model.
Figure 13: Writing case (EndoR). Response generated by the EndoR-trained model under the same prompt.
Figure 8: Reward case study (Model output from SFT).
Figure 7: Reward case study (Prompt).
Figure 9: Reward case study. The table reports EndoR and TCER scores for each line, ∆ = TCER− EndoR, and whether the line is selected by the closed-source judge panel.
Figure 15: English writing case (SFT). Response generated by the SFT-only model.
Figure 14: Writing case (TCER). Response generated by the TCER-trained model under the same prompt.

Table 1: Performance comparison on writing benchmarks. We evaluate models across LongBench-Write, HelloBench subsets, and WritingBench domains. Starting from Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, each training stage (SFT, RL-EndoR, RL-TCER) demonstrates progressive improvements.

Table 6: Hyperparameter sensitivity analysis on the DeepWriter dataset with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct. Results show win rates or benchmark scores across LongBench (LB), HelloBench subsets (HB-A: Open QA, HB-B: Text Generation), and WritingBench domains (WB-A through WB-F representing different professional writing categories). The optimal configuration (k = 3, λ = 2) achieves consistent improvements across all metrics.
